entry.title: Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando
entry.content:
ASUG110: Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire: Join the Exclusive ASUG Pre-Conference on the New SAP Services & Support Portfolio

SAP is entering a new era of services and support—one that is simpler, more intelligent, and designed to help organizations move faster toward cloud transformation and AI-driven innovation. To help customers understand and fully leverage this new approach, ASUG and SAP are hosting an exclusive full-day pre-conference at SAP Sapphire:

“Unlocking Transformational Value: A Practical Introduction to the New SAP Services & Support Portfolio.”

This immersive event brings together SAP leaders, product experts, and customers to walk through the modernized SAP Services & Support portfolio, explain the new tiered Success Plans, and demonstrate how organizations can accelerate cloud adoption, improve operations, and drive measurable business outcomes. With breakfast, lunch, expert sessions, and an exclusive showfloor tour, the day provides both strategic insight and practical guidance for anyone responsible for SAP transformation. Whether you are planning a cloud migration, optimizing hybrid landscapes, or exploring how AI can transform operations, this session will help you understand how to maximize value from your SAP investment.
Register Now!

What You’ll Learn

During this full-day experience, participants will explore:

  • The new SAP Success Plans: Foundational, Advanced, and Max

  • How AI-enabled services improve productivity and automation

  • Ways to optimize SAP operations and governance

  • How Development Services and co-innovation accelerate innovation

  • How Application Management and Sovereign Cloud support modern enterprise requirements

You’ll leave with practical guidance for building your SAP success strategy for 2026 and beyond. Register Now!

Full Agenda May 11 | Orlando

Note: Session agenda, elements, and speakers are subject to change.

8:30–8:45 AM — Welcome

Welcome & Setting the Stage

Speakers:
Geoff Scott, CEO and Chief Community Champion, ASUG
Stefan Steinle, EVP and Head of Global Customer Support, SAP SE

Kick off the day with ASUG and SAP leadership as they outline why SAP simplified its Services & Support portfolio. Learn what customers asked for and how the new model supports cloud adoption, AI-driven innovation, and predictable, business-aligned outcomes.

8:45–9:15 AM — Keynote Session

The New SAP Services & Support Portfolio: Simpler, Smarter, Predictable

Speakers:
Thomas Pfiester, Head of Customer Engagement & Adoption and Member of the Extended Board of SAP SE
Stefan Steinle, EVP and Head Global Customer Support, SAP
Uwe Grigoleit, SVP, Head of SAP Customer Evolution and Portfolio

Discover the structure and strategy behind SAP’s new Services & Support portfolio. This keynote introduces SAP Success Plans (Foundational, Advanced, and Max) alongside Development Services, Application Management, Professional Services, and SAP Sovereign Cloud. Learn how AI-enabled services simplify operations, increase predictability, and accelerate adoption, innovation, and business value.

9:15–9:25 AM — Break

9:25–9:55 AM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Foundational to get started and achieve continuous value with confidence

Speakers:
Rohan Patel, Global Head of Support, SAP Procurement
Jens Bernotat, Global Vice President Strategy, Portfolio, Global Customer Support, SAP

Learn what’s included in the SAP Foundational Success Plan, including SAP Enterprise Support, digital guidance, SAP Cloud ALM, and best practices. This session highlights how organizations can onboard faster, maintain system stability, and continuously improve operations across their SAP cloud landscape.

9:55–10:25 AM — Spotlight Session

Transforming the Cloud Journey with SAP Cloud ALM and Next-Generation Agents

Speakers:
Björn Braemer, SVP & Program Executive, Customer Evolution Program, SAP
Thorsten Spihlmann, Head of Business Development for Data Transformation, SAP

SAP Cloud ALM is evolving into a Transformation Execution Platform, helping organizations accelerate cloud transformation. Discover how an enhanced AI toolchain and agentic AI capabilities support configuration, migration, data preparation, and automated testing—reducing manual effort and enabling faster, smarter transformation journeys.

10:25–11:00 AM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Advanced for Guided, Predictable Transformation

Speakers:
Peter Roberts, SVP & Chief Product Officer, Advanced Success Plan, SAP
Sachin Vaidya ,Director – Enterprise Commercial Solutions, Pfizer

The Advanced Success Plan provides expert-guided engagement for complex transformation programs. Learn how tailored guidance, architectural reviews, readiness checks, and proactive insights help ensure predictable adoption and business-aligned outcomes across regions and teams.

11:00–11:35 PM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Max for Strategic Partnership and Business-Critical Success

Speakers:
Pallavi Kothuri, Marathon Petroleum
Sebastien Denes, Global Vice President, Chief Product Officer Max Success Plan, SAP

Explore SAP’s highest level of engagement with the Max Success Plan. Learn how dedicated experts, proactive governance, AI-driven insights, and mission-critical operations support help enterprises protect business continuity, accelerate innovation, and drive strategic outcomes at scale.

11:35–11:50 PM — Point of View: 15 minutes with Geoff Scott and Thomas Saueressig

Speakers:
Geoff Scott, CEO and Chief Community Champion, ASUG
Thomas Saueressig, Member of the Executive Board of SAP SE, Head of Customer Services & Delivery

Join Geoff Scott and Thomas Saueressig for a 15-minute “Point of View” session as they reflect on SAP’s new Success Plans. Gain their insights on how these plans are shapingcustomer outcomes, driving engagement, and supporting business transformation. A brief, focused conversation packed with perspective and actionable takeaways.

11:50 AM–12:50 PM — Lunch Break

12:50–1:30 PM — Sponsor Session: Intelligent Change Management in action

Speakers:
David Lees, CTO, Basis Technologies

Discover how Intelligent Change Management uses agentic AI and SAP solutions to design, assess, and deploy the right change at the pace the business demands. Follow a business request from SAP Cloud ALM to Klario for AI-driven assessment and through to deployment with ActiveDiscover and ActiveControl—compressing cycles, reducing risk, and delivering measurable business impact.

1:30–2:15 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Co-Innovating with SAP's Development Services: From AI Ideas to Impact

Speakers:
Sindhu Gangadharan, Head, Customer Innovation Services; MD, SAP Labs India
Shobana Achar, Head ERP Operation, Genentech
Martha Greene, Executive Director, Channel Operations, Genentech

Discover how SAP Development Services partners with customers to build tailored solutions—from generative AI use cases to agentic AI scenarios. This session includes a real-world example demonstrating how custom AI innovation improved efficiency, decision-making, and measurable business outcomes.

2:15–3:00 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Next Generation Application Management for Predictive, Intelligent, and Automated Operations

Speakers:
Lalit Patil, EVP & Chief Technology Officer, SAP
Vinay Hiriganahalli, SVP, Head of Cloud Application Services, SAP

Learn how SAP Application Management is transforming operations from reactive support to predictive, AI-driven optimization. Through artificial intelligence, machine learning, and cognitive automation, organizations can move beyond issue resolution toward proactive performance management and intelligent operations.

3:00–3:15 PM — Break

3:15–3:45 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Secure your future with SAP Sovereign Cloud 

Speaker:
Peter Keller, VP, Sovereign Cloud Architecture, SAP
Barb Brown, Enterprise Architect, Lockheed Martin Corporation
Katy Sinclair, Integration Architect, Lockheed Martin Corporation

Discover how SAP Sovereign Cloud capabilities can cater to the unique needs of public sector and regulated industries. Learn about these capabilities, which fulfill advancedcompliance requirements across our four digital sovereignty dimensions: data, operational, legal and technical sovereignty. These dimensions address heightened digital sovereigntyneeds enabling companies to leverage the significant benefits of the cloud.  

3:45–4:30 PM — Strategy Session

Build Your SAP Success Strategy: Practical Guidance and Roadmap 2026

Speakers:
Uwe Grigoleit, SVP, Head of SAP Customer Evolution and Portfolio, SAP
Patrick Wells, Global Head of SAP Services Portfolio Evolution, SAP

This practical session helps you translate insights from the day into a clear SAP engagement strategy.

Learn how to:

  • Match business priorities with the right Success Plan

  • Identify ROI drivers

  • Apply best practices for adoption

Build a practical roadmap for 2026

Participants will leave with a template to assess their current setup and define their future SAP success strategy.
Register Now!

Exclusive Bonus: Guided SAP Sapphire Showfloor Tour & F1 Experience

4:30–6:00 PM

Discover Sapphire Like an Insider

Wrap up the day with a curated, expert-led tour of the SAP Sapphire showfloor. Navigating Sapphire can be overwhelming—this guided experience ensures you don’t miss the innovations and demos most relevant to your SAP journey.

Tour highlights include:

  • Exclusive access to the SAP Experience Center

  • Artificial Intelligence innovations

  • Data & Analytics

  • Business Applications

  • Technology Platform

  • Business Transformation

  • Industry Center

  • Services & Support Center

And to finish the day: Experience the Formula 1 racing simulator and connect with SAP experts in an interactive setting. Register Now!

Why You Should Attend

This ASUG pre-conference offers a unique opportunity to learn directly from SAP leaders and customers about the future of services and support.

Participants will gain:

✔ A clear understanding of SAP’s new Services & Support portfolio
✔ Practical insights into AI-driven operations and transformation
✔ Real customer experiences and best practices
✔ Direct access to SAP experts
✔ A structured strategy for maximizing value from SAP investments

Seats are limited — secure your spot and start SAP Sapphire with insights that will shape your transformation journey. Register Now!

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: A Practitioner's Setup Guide
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  PRACTICAL GUIDE  |  SAP BTP  |  SAP AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: A Practitioner's Setup Guide

Step-by-step configuration from BTP entitlements to your first foundation model API call — including the friction points that aren't in the official documentation.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP BTP & AI Core  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP Generative AI Hub gives you governed, auditable access to GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, and SAP's own models from a single BTP service. Getting there requires navigating BTP entitlements, AI Core service instance setup, resource group configuration, and API authentication — none of which is as straightforward as the documentation suggests. This guide covers the complete setup path and the specific friction points that will slow you down if you don't know about them.

What the Generative AI Hub Actually Is

SAP's Generative AI Hub is the access layer within SAP AI Core that gives you a single, governed interface to multiple foundation models — GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI, Claude from Anthropic, Gemini from Google, Mistral, SAP-RPT-1, and others — without building separate integrations to each provider. It handles authentication, usage tracking, rate limiting, and compliance logging in a way that meets enterprise governance requirements.

For SAP architects and data engineers, the key value proposition is straightforward: instead of managing separate API keys, separate billing accounts, and separate compliance considerations for each AI provider, the Generative AI Hub abstracts all of that into a single BTP service with consistent access patterns, audit trails, and cost visibility.

What the documentation does not prepare you for is how much configuration stands between you and your first working API call. This guide covers every step — including the ones that aren't clearly documented — so you can get from zero to a working foundation model integration without the friction we went through.

BEFORE YOU START 

Prerequisites and What You Actually Need

Before beginning setup, confirm you have the following. Missing any of these is the most common reason setup stalls before it starts:

  • A BTP global account with available entitlements — a trial account works for initial experimentation but has model and quota limitations
  • A BTP subaccount in a supported region — AI Core availability varies by region and not all models are available in all regions (covered in detail below)
  • Space Manager or Organization Manager role in your BTP subaccount — standard Developer roles are insufficient for service instance creation
  • Access to the BTP Cockpit — your entry point for all entitlement and service configuration

Region matters more than you might expect. GPT-4o, Claude, and Gemini availability through the Generative AI Hub is not uniform across all BTP regions. US East, EU West, and AP regions have the broadest model availability. If you set up AI Core in an unsupported region, you will successfully create the service instance but find that certain models are simply not visible in the Hub. Check SAP's current model availability matrix before choosing your subaccount region.

STEP 1 OF 5 

BTP Entitlement Setup

Adding the AI Core Entitlement

Navigate to your BTP global account and open the Entitlements section. This is where most teams hit their first friction point — the AI Core service and the Generative AI Hub are separate entitlements and both need to be explicitly added before you can create a service instance.

In the BTP Cockpit, go to Entitlements → Entity Assignments → select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.

Search for and add the following:

  • SAP AI Core — select the Extended service plan (not the Free tier, which has significant model and quota restrictions)
  • SAP AI Launchpad — needed to access the Generative AI Hub UI; easy to miss if you assume the Hub is part of AI Core itself

The most common entitlement mistake: adding SAP AI Core but not SAP AI Launchpad. AI Core is the runtime service. AI Launchpad is the UI that gives you access to the Generative AI Hub prompt editor and model catalog. Without AI Launchpad, you can make API calls but you cannot use the visual interface for prompt experimentation. Add both entitlements before proceeding.

Quota Allocation

After adding the entitlements, allocate quota to your subaccount. The default quota allocation for AI Core is zero — you must explicitly set it. In the Entitlements screen, set the quota for the Extended plan to at least 1 unit to enable service instance creation.

Quota and billing transparency is a genuine friction point here. The Generative AI Hub bills on a token consumption basis per model, but the BTP entitlement setup does not show you projected costs or usage limits upfront. Before running any workloads in production, contact your SAP account team to understand the cost model for your specific contract — trial and CPEA accounts have different billing mechanics.

 

Step

Action

Detail

1

Open BTP Cockpit

Navigate to your global account → Subaccounts → select your subaccount

2

Open Entitlements

Entitlements → Entity Assignments → Configure Entitlements

3

Add AI Core

Add Service Plans → search 'SAP AI Core' → select Extended plan

4

Add AI Launchpad

Add Service Plans → search 'SAP AI Launchpad' → select Standard plan

5

Set quota

Set AI Core Extended plan quota to minimum 1 unit → Save

STEP 2 OF 5 

Creating the AI Core Service Instance

Service Instance Creation

With entitlements in place, navigate to your BTP subaccount → Services → Service Marketplace → search for SAP AI Core → select the Extended plan → Create.

During instance creation you will be prompted to provide a JSON configuration parameter. For standard Generative AI Hub access, the default configuration is sufficient — do not skip this step by leaving it empty, as certain model access features require the configuration to be present even if it is set to default values.

{
  "serviceName": "aicore",
  "description": "AI Core instance for Generative AI Hub access"
}

Creating a Service Key

After the service instance is created, you need a service key to generate the credentials your applications will use for API authentication. In the service instance detail view, select Service Keys → Create → provide a name → Create.

The service key JSON contains the clientid, clientsecret, url (for OAuth token endpoint), and serviceurls.AI_API_URL (your AI Core API base URL). Save this immediately and store it securely — you will need all four values for API authentication.

API authentication is more complex than a simple API key. AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow. Your application must first call the token endpoint with the clientid and clientsecret to obtain a bearer token, then include that token in each AI Core API call. Tokens expire and must be refreshed. Many teams expect a simple API key pattern and are surprised by the OAuth requirement. Plan your authentication implementation before writing any AI logic.

STEP 3 OF 5 

Resource Group Configuration

What Resource Groups Are and Why They Matter

A resource group in SAP AI Core is a logical isolation boundary for AI workloads — models, deployments, and executions run within a resource group context. It is not obvious from the documentation that resource group configuration is a prerequisite for accessing the Generative AI Hub, but it is. Without a configured resource group, model deployments will not be visible even after AI Core and AI Launchpad are correctly set up.

Resource group configuration is done through the AI Core API, not through the BTP Cockpit UI — which is another friction point teams don't anticipate. You will need to make a direct API call to create your resource group using the service key credentials you obtained in Step 2.

Creating the Resource Group via API

First, obtain an OAuth token using your service key credentials:

# Step 1: Get OAuth token
curl -X POST '<token_url>/oauth/token' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  -d 'grant_type=client_credentials' \
  -d 'client_id=<clientid>' \
  -d 'client_secret=<clientsecret>'

Then create your resource group using the bearer token:

# Step 2: Create resource group
curl -X PUT '<AI_API_URL>/v2/admin/resourceGroups' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"resourceGroupId": "default"}'

Once the resource group is created, return to the AI Launchpad. You should now see the resource group available in the dropdown. Select it and navigate to the Generative AI Hub — the model catalog will now be visible and accessible.

STEP 4 OF 5 

Accessing the Generative AI Hub and Available Models

Navigating to the Hub

With AI Launchpad subscribed and your resource group configured, navigate to your BTP subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Launchpad → Go to Application. Select your AI Core connection, choose your resource group, and open the Generative AI Hub from the left navigation.

The Hub has two primary sections you will use immediately: the Chat interface for interactive prompt testing and the Prompt Editor for structured experimentation with system prompts, parameters, and response comparison across models.

Available Foundation Models

The following models were available and tested during our setup. Model availability evolves — check the SAP AI Core release notes for the current catalog as new models are added regularly:

 

Model

Provider

Type

Best For

GPT-4o

Azure OpenAI

General LLM

Complex reasoning, code generation, document analysis

GPT-4o mini

Azure OpenAI

General LLM

Cost-efficient tasks, high-volume processing

Claude 3.5 Sonnet

Anthropic

General LLM

Long context, nuanced analysis, structured output

Claude 3 Haiku

Anthropic

General LLM

Fast, cost-efficient, high-volume classification

Gemini 1.5 Pro

Google

General LLM

Multimodal, very long context window

Mistral Large

Mistral AI

General LLM

European data residency, strong multilingual

Mistral Small

Mistral AI

General LLM

Cost-efficient European-hosted option

SAP-RPT-1

SAP

Tabular/structured

Numerical predictions on SAP tabular data

Not all models are available in all regions. If a model you expect to see is missing from your Hub catalog, the most likely cause is that your AI Core instance is in a region where that model is not yet deployed. This is not always clearly surfaced in the UI — the model simply does not appear rather than showing as unavailable. Check SAP's regional availability documentation if your expected models are missing.

STEP 5 OF 5 

Making Your First API Call

Using the Prompt Editor for Experimentation

Before writing any code, use the Generative AI Hub's built-in Prompt Editor to verify your setup is working and to experiment with models. The Prompt Editor allows you to set a system prompt, write a user message, adjust temperature and max token parameters, and compare responses across multiple models side by side.

This is the fastest way to validate that your entitlements, service instance, resource group, and model access are all correctly configured before investing time in application code.

Direct API Integration

For application integration, the AI Core API follows a consistent pattern across all models. After obtaining an OAuth token, construct your request to the chat completions endpoint:

import requests

# Step 1: Get OAuth bearer token
token_response = requests.post(
    f'{token_url}/oauth/token',
    data={
        'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
        'client_id': client_id,
        'client_secret': client_secret
    }
)
bearer_token = token_response.json()['access_token']

# Step 2: Call foundation model via AI Core API
headers = {
    'Authorization': f'Bearer {bearer_token}',
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
    'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'
}

payload = {
    'model': 'gpt-4o',
    'messages': [
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are an SAP data analyst.'},
        {'role': 'user', 'content': 'Summarize this SAP exception log: ...'}
    ],
    'max_tokens': 500,
    'temperature': 0.3
}

response = requests.post(
    f'{ai_api_url}/v2/inference/deployments/<deployment_id>/chat/completions',
    headers=headers,
    json=payload
)
print(response.json()['choices'][0]['message']['content'])

The deployment_id in the API endpoint URL is model-specific and changes when SAP updates model versions. Retrieve current deployment IDs programmatically from the AI Core API rather than hardcoding them — hardcoded deployment IDs are the most common cause of sudden API failures after a model version update.

Switching Between Models

The same API pattern works across all models in the Generative AI Hub — only the model parameter and deployment_id change. This is the core value of the Hub's abstraction layer: your application code does not need to change when you switch from GPT-4o to Claude or Mistral. The authentication, request structure, and response format are consistent.

The Friction Points: A Summary

For teams setting up the Generative AI Hub for the first time, these are the specific issues that will slow you down if you don't know about them upfront:

 

Step

Action

Detail

1

Missing AI Launchpad entitlement

AI Core alone is not sufficient — add SAP AI Launchpad separately or you will have no UI access to the Hub

2

Zero default quota

BTP allocates zero quota by default — explicitly set quota to at least 1 unit after adding the entitlement

3

Resource group not created

Models are invisible in the Hub until a resource group exists — create via API call before opening the Launchpad

4

OAuth not simple API key

AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials — implement token refresh logic before writing AI application code

5

Regional model gaps

Models missing from your catalog are likely unavailable in your region — check availability before choosing subaccount region

6

Hardcoded deployment IDs

Deployment IDs change with model updates — retrieve programmatically to avoid silent failures after SAP updates

What You Can Build From Here

With the Generative AI Hub correctly configured, the path to production SAP AI applications becomes significantly clearer. The governed, multi-model access layer means your team can experiment across GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, and Mistral without managing separate provider relationships, and switch models based on performance, cost, or compliance requirements without rewriting application code.

The natural next steps from this setup are connecting the Generative AI Hub to your HANA Cloud vector store for RAG-based applications on SAP data, building SAP Joule extensions that leverage foundation model capabilities, and integrating AI Core into your BTP CAP application workflows for intelligent enterprise applications.

The setup friction is real but it is a one-time cost. Once the entitlements, service instance, resource group, and authentication are in place, the development experience is significantly smoother than managing individual AI provider integrations.

If you hit any of the friction points listed above or encounter issues not covered here, drop a comment below. These are the setups where community knowledge compounds quickly — your specific error message or configuration issue is likely something another practitioner has already solved.

entry.title: Incident Management with AI Adapter and ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction:

With the latest capabilities in SAP Integration Suite, organizations can make incident handling much smarter and faster. By combining the AI Adapter with the ServiceNow Adapter, integration flows can automatically analyze errors and create incident tickets without human intervention.

Instead of developers or support teams manually reviewing logs and raising tickets, Generative AI can interpret the error, understand the issue, and automatically trigger the creation of incident in ServiceNow. This makes the entire exception-handling process more efficient and responsive.

By introducing AI into the integration workflow, organizations can significantly reduce manual effort and lower the Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) for critical integration failures.

 

Disclaimer: This blog includes extensive step-by-step explanations and several screenshots, which may make the article longer than usual. 

 

Concept Overview:

The architecture follows three simple steps:

1. Capture the Error Messages

It’s best to create a generic iflow to handle the entire process of automating the ticket creation in ServiceNow from the error generated in other iflows to be directed using JMS or processDirect adapters

2. Analyze and Generate the Prompt

Using the AI Adapter, the error payload is sent to a Large Language Model (LLM). The model analyzes the stack trace or error message and generates:

  • A simplified explanation of the issue
  • Possible root causes
  • Suggested resolution steps

3. Raise Ticket using ServiceNow Adapter

The enriched information is then sent to the ServiceNow Adapter, which automatically creates a structured incident ticket containing the AI-generated analysis.

AI Adapter Variants:

The AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite currently supports two variants:

  1. AICore-GenAIHub
  2. Custom-OpenAI-REST

AICore-GenAIHub

The AICore-GenAIHub variant integrates with the SAP AI Core service via the SAP Generative AI Hub. SAP GenAI Setup

The Generative AI Hub provides a central gateway to multiple generative AI models from different providers, allowing organizations to consume and manage AI models through SAP’s AI infrastructure on BTP. This enables us to switch between

To use this variant:

  • SAP AI Core service must be configured in the BTP tenant.
  • It supports OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication
  • The service is available only in enterprise or extended environments, not in trial accounts.

Since this setup is not available in the trial landscape, this article will focus on the Custom-OpenAI-REST variant.

Custom-OpenAI-REST

The Custom-OpenAI-REST variant allows integration with AI models that are not hosted or managed by SAP.

This option is used when an AI platform exposes OpenAI-compatible APIs but is not directly supported by SAP’s predefined adapter configurations.

Key characteristics:

  • Configuration Type: Basic or Advanced
  • Connects to OpenAI-compatible endpoints
  • Uses standard REST APIs such as
    • /chat/completions
    • /responses
  • Supports API Key based authentication using Secure Parameters

This enables integration with external AI providers such as OpenAI, Google, or other platforms that expose OpenAI-style APIs.

Prerequisites:

Before building this integration scenario, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. Google AI Studio account

    Choose an AI model and generate an API key.

    In this example, we will use the Gemini 1.5 Flash model available via Google AI Studio.

  2. ServiceNow account and Instance

    A ServiceNow account and an active instance are required to create and manage incident tickets through the ServiceNow adapter.

Note: Since I’m using the Gemini AI model, I will configure the adapter using the Advanced configuration type. I will also explain the Basic configuration type and highlight the key differences between the two.

Subscribe to the Gemini Flash Model

First, log in to Google AI Studio and generate an API key that will be used in our IFlow.

After successfully logging in, navigate to the Dashboard section to proceed with the API key creation.

 

Next, go to the Projects section and create a new project by providing an appropriate name. Navigate to the Keys section and create a new key.

Click on the Create API key

Your key has now been generated. Make sure to save it, as it will be required later.

Alternatively, you can click on “Copy cURL quickstart”, which will provide the complete URL, API key, and request body, as shown below.

curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent" \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-H 'X-goog-api-key: <Your API Key>' \
-X POST \
-d '{
"contents": [
{
"parts": [
{
"text": "Explain how AI works in a few words"
}
]
}
]
}'

ServiceNow Instance Creation:

Now let’s set up the ServiceNow Developer account and create an instance. Visit the ServiceNow Developers page and create an account using your e-mail address. Once you verify your e-mail, you will be redirected to the following screen.

Select the instance of your choice. In my case, I’m selecting the Zurich instance.

Your personal developer instance has now been created, and you will be able to see the generated username and password. We will use these credentials in the ServiceNow Adapter for Basic Authentication

Now that we have the credentials, we need the API details to create an incident in ServiceNow. Log in to your instance using the REST API Explorer | ServiceNow. Once you open it, you will be able to see the list of available resources.

  1. API Name: Select Table API.
  2. Choose the Create a Record (POST) option.
  3. Copy the URL and replace {tablename} with the actual table name (in our case, the incident table).
  4. Scroll down and select the fields that need to be included in the request body.

Note: In ServiceNow, all data is stored in tables. It is also possible to create an empty incident without passing any data in the request body.

As shown below, a ticket is created even when no data is provided in the request body.

To view the list of created incidents, go to Workspaces, search for Incidents, select All Results, and under Service Decks, click on Incidents.

IFlow Process Flow Overview

Finally, let’s take a look at our IFlow and understand how we will use the above setup in our adapters.

Integration Flow

As the Sender, we have used the HTTPS adapter and tested the IFlow using Postman. Based on the requirements, this can be changed - for example, the JMS or ProcessDirect adapters can also be used instead of the HTTPS adapter.

Next comes the key part. In the Content Modifier, I have explicitly defined the prompt that will be sent to the AI model.

AI Model Prompt Content Payload

You can customize the prompt based on your requirements. Now, let’s look at the AI Adapter and the configuration required for the Custom OpenAI REST variant.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Use the base URL of the endpoint.
  • API Key Alias: Specify the secure parameter created in the Security Material. This secure parameter stores the API key for the AI model.

AI Adapter - Connection Settings

In the Processing tab configuration, since I’m not using an OpenAI model like ChatGPT—which provides standard endpoints such as /chat/completions or /responses (we’ll look at this later)—I have selected the Advanced type. This option provides more configuration parameters compared to the Basic type.

  1. HTTP Method – Since we are triggering the process to create an incident, select the POST method.

  2. Relative URL – Copy the relative path from the actual endpoint URL and paste it here:

    /v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent

  3. Query – This is mostly used with the GET method. In our case, it is not applicable, so leave it as the default.

Headers

I created header parameters in the Content Modifier named X-goog-api-key to pass the API key and also added Content-Type: application/json.

Content Modifier Header Properties

Processing Tab:

AI Adapter - Advanced Configuration Type Settings

Let’s also discuss the Basic configuration type.

AI Adapter Basic Configuration Type Setting

 

 Parameter NameDescription
Configuration TypeDetermines how the AI adapter is configured. Basic allows configuration through predefined UI fields such as model and prompt, while Advanced provides more flexibility to define the entire request payload manually.
CategorySpecifies the type of AI capability to be used. Available options include Chat Completions, Responses, and Platform. Each category represents a different set of AI APIs used for conversational AI, structured responses, or advanced platform capabilities such as embeddings and image generation.
Entity TypeRepresents the specific AI resource within the selected category. 
Operation

Defines the action to be executed using the selected entity. The available operations depend on the selected Category and Entity Type.

For example, in Chat Completions, the operation is typically Create a model response for a given conversation. Other entities in the platform category may support operations like create, retrieve, update, or delete resources.

Request Payload Source

Determines how the request payload (prompt and parameters) is passed to the AI model.

Options include:

1. UI Configurable – Allows the prompt and model to be defined directly in the adapter configuration.

2. Exchange Body – Allows the request payload to be dynamically provided from the integration flow, typically using a Content Modifier or Groovy script, and passed as the message body.

Model (when UI Configurable is selected)

Specifies the AI model used to generate the response. The list contains the models available in the configured AI service (for example, GPT-based models).

Prompt (when UI Configurable is selected)

Allows the user to directly define the prompt or instruction that will be sent to the AI model. It can include instructions, questions, or contextual information required for the AI interaction.

Difference between /chat/completions and /response endpoints in OpenAI API

Feature/chat/completions/responses

Purpose

Designed specifically for chat-based interactionsA unified endpoint for all types of AI responses
Input FormatUses messages array (system, user, assistant)Uses a more flexible input structure
Future scopeOlder approach (still supported)Recommended modern API by OpenAI
FunctionalitiesPrimarily text/chatSupports text, images, audio, and tool use

Example:

1. /chat/completions

POST /v1/chat/completions
{
  "model":"gpt-4o-mini",
  "messages": [
    {"role":"system", "content":"You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role":"user", "content":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"}
  ]
}

2. /responses

POST /v1/responses
{
  "model":"gpt-4.1-mini",
  "input":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"
}

Key Takeaway

  • /chat/completions → Built specifically for chat-style conversations.
  • /responses → A new unified API that works for multiple AI capabilities and is the recommended approach for new integrations.

As the next step, I created a Groovy script to process the response from the AI and extract and format the relevant content required. If you look at my prompt, I asked the model to return the response in JSON format. However, inside the Content tag, the model returned something like this:

{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "content": {
        "parts": [
          {
            "text": "```json\n{\n  \"ErrorDescription\": \"Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.\",\n  \"PossibleCauses\": \"Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.\",\n  \"ResolutionSteps\": \"Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.\",\n  \"SupportGuidance\": \"If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.\",\n  \"IncidentMetadata\": {\n    \"state\": \"In Progress\",\n    \"incident_state\": \"Investigating\",\n    \"impact\": \"2\"\n  }\n}\n```"
          }
        ],
        "role": "model"
      },
      "finishReason": "STOP",
      "index": 0
    }
  ],
  "usageMetadata": {
    "promptTokenCount": 257,
    "candidatesTokenCount": 266,
    "totalTokenCount": 1366,
    "promptTokensDetails": [
      {
        "modality": "TEXT",
        "tokenCount": 257
      }
    ],
    "thoughtsTokenCount": 843
  },
  "modelVersion": "gemini-2.5-flash",
  "responseId": "XvevabT6KcDcqtsP_8GP8Q0"
}

Next, I used a Groovy script to extract only the JSON content present inside the text tag. The output after the Groovy script will contain only the required JSON content. Now, we can use the JSON to XML Converter to transform the below payload into an XML structure.

{
  "ErrorDescription": "Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.",
  "PossibleCauses": "Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.",
  "ResolutionSteps": "Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.",
  "SupportGuidance": "If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.",
  "IncidentMetadata": {
    "state": "In Progress",
    "incident_state": "Investigating",
    "impact": "2"
  }
}

In the next Content Modifier, I created a few properties with the Source Type set to XPath. These properties are then used to construct the request payload that will be sent to ServiceNow.

Custom Properties created in Content Modifier

Content Modifier Request Payload for ServiceNow Ticket Creation

Content Modifier Body:

{
    "short_description": "${property.short_description}",
    "description": "${property.PossibleCauses}, ${property.ResolutionSteps}, ${property.SupportGuidance}",
    "state": "${property.state}",
    "incident_state": "${property.incident_state}",
    "impact": "${property.impact}"
}

Finally, let’s look at the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Copy and paste your ServiceNow instance URL.
  • Authentication: Select Basic Authentication.
  • Credentials: Enter the user credentials that you created in the Security Material.

ServiceNow Connection Tab Settings

In the Processing tab, we are presented with several additional parameters to configure.

ServiceNow Adapter Processing Tab Configurations

API Version - You can choose from different REST endpoint versions. If you are not certain which version to use, it is recommended to leave it as the default value.

Operation - Several operations are available. Since we are creating an incident, select the Create Object operation.

Table Name - Incident because that were all the ticket are stored in ServiceNow

Display Values - The Display Value option determines how reference field values are returned in the response payload. 

In ServiceNow tables, many fields store internal system identifiers (Sys IDs) instead of human-readable values. The Display Value setting controls whether the adapter returns:

  • the raw system value,
  • the human-readable display value, or
  • both the system value and display value.

Access Data Across Domain - This option allows the adapter to retrieve records from multiple domains within a ServiceNow instance.

  • If disabled, the adapter accesses data only from the current domain.

  • If enabled, it can retrieve records from all available domains, such as Global, IT, or Infrastructure.

Response Field - Specifies the fields or properties to be returned in the ServiceNow response. If multiple fields are required, they should be separated using commas.

Render Response For - Available options are Both, Mobile, and Desktop. This setting determines how the response is formatted based on the client type, ensuring compatibility across different platforms.

Suppress System Fields Auto Generation - This option prevents ServiceNow from automatically generating certain system fields when a record is created. Examples include: sys_id, sys_created_on

Display Raw Values - When Display Raw Values is enabled, the API returns the exact value stored in the database. By default, ServiceNow returns data in a formatted or human-readable format.

Exclude Reference Link - In ServiceNow responses, reference fields often include a link to the related resource. When Exclude Reference Link is enabled, the response returns only the value and removes the reference link.

Let’s now look at the actual difference in the response when all these properties are enabled.

Response Without Enabling the Mentioned Fields

Response when the Mentioned Fields are enabled

This is how these options affect the ServiceNow response payload.

Under the Format section, we have Request and Response options. These are quite straightforward, so I’ll skip a detailed explanation here.

Note: The available properties may vary depending on the selected operation. For more details about the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter, refer to the official documentation. ServiceNow Receiver Adapter | SAP Help Portal

Important Note: Make sure your ServiceNow instance is active and running. If the instance is in sleep mode while testing the IFlow, you may receive a response such as “Instance Hibernating Page.”

End to End testing from Postman

In the first Content Modifier, the error tag is captured and stored in a property. In the message body, the expression ${property.errorMsg} is used so that the prompt dynamically includes the specific error content. This ensures that for each error, the AI model receives a different prompt based on the error message.

Response: 

You can see an incident is created with a number "INC0010027". Now, let’s check ServiceNow to see where the incident is created. As expected, a new entry appears in the incident table.

Conclusion:

By integrating the AI Adapter with ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite, we can significantly improve how incidents are identified and created within enterprise landscapes. Instead of manually analyzing logs and raising tickets, the integration flow can capture errors, send them to an AI model for analysis, and automatically generate a structured incident in ServiceNow.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 2 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  GENERATIVE AI HUB

SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks

Updated March 2026: Corrected environment section — Jupyter notebooks run in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)

Five practical SAP use cases with working Python code — across SAP AI Launchpad notebooks, local scripts, and BTP applications — using the generative-ai-hub-sdk for clean, production-ready authentication.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Once your Generative AI Hub is configured (see Part 1), the generative-ai-hub-sdk Python package handles OAuth token management automatically — removing the biggest friction point from direct API integration. This article covers SDK installation and configuration across three environments (AI Launchpad notebooks, local Python scripts, BTP applications), followed by five working code examples for real SAP data tasks: exception log summarization, HANA query analysis, ABAP/SQL code generation, master data classification, and structured data extraction from SAP documents.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Part 1 covered the complete setup path from BTP entitlements to your first API call. If you haven't configured your AI Core instance and resource group yet, start there before continuing.

This part focuses on practical Python integration — the code patterns you'll use for real SAP workloads, across the three environments where SAP practitioners most commonly run Python against the Hub.

 

Environment

Best For

Key Consideration

AI Launchpad Notebooks

Interactive development, rapid prototyping

Credentials pre-configured — fastest to start

Local Python Script

Automation, scheduled jobs, local development

Requires .env file with service key credentials

BTP Application

Production workloads, CAP integration, APIs

Credentials via BTP environment variables or secrets

SDK INSTALLATION & CONFIGURATION 

Installing and Configuring the generative-ai-hub-sdk

Why the SDK Over Direct API Calls

Part 1 showed the raw OAuth 2.0 flow using direct API calls — which is useful to understand what's happening under the hood. For any real workload, the generative-ai-hub-sdk is the better path. It handles token acquisition, token refresh, retry logic, and deployment ID resolution automatically. Your application code focuses on the task, not the authentication plumbing.

Installation

# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
pip install generative-ai-hub-sdk
 
# Optional but recommended — for HANA connection examples later
pip install hdbcli pandas python-dotenv

Configuration: AI Launchpad Notebooks

  ▶  Environment: SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) — Jupyter Notebooks 

Setting Up a BAS Dev Space for Jupyter

SAP Business Application Studio is the browser-based IDE available on BTP that supports Jupyter notebooks via the built-in Jupyter and Python extensions. This is the SAP-native interactive notebook environment for Python development — not SAP AI Launchpad, which does not provide a notebook interface.

BAS is available to all BTP customers and trial users. No additional entitlement beyond BAS subscription is required to run Jupyter notebooks.

Dev Space Setup — Step by Step

Step 1 — Create a Dev Space:

  • Open SAP Business Application Studio from your BTP subaccount
  • Click Create Dev Space
  • Name it (e.g. GenAIHub_Dev) and select SAP HANA Native Application as the type
  • Under Additional SAP Extensions, select Python Tools
  • Click Create Dev Space and wait for status to show Running
  • Click the dev space name to open it

 

Step 2 — Install pip and required packages:

Open a terminal in BAS (F1 → Open Terminal) and run:

# Install pip if not already available
curl https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py > get-pip.py && python3 get-pip.py
echo "export PATH=/home/user/.local/bin:$PATH" >> ~/.bashrc && source ~/.bashrc
 
# Create a virtual environment for your project
python3 -m venv ~/.venv/genai
source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate
 
# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK and Jupyter kernel
pip install generative-ai-hub-sdk ipykernel hdbcli pandas python-dotenv
python -m ipykernel install --user --name genai --display-name 'GenAI Hub'

Step 3 — Create the AI Core credentials file:

Create .aicore-config.json in your home directory with your AI Core service key values:

{
  "AICORE_AUTH_URL": "https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": "<clientid from service key>",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": "<clientsecret from service key>",
  "AICORE_BASE_URL": "<AI_API_URL from service key>",
  "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default"
}

Step 4 — Create and run a notebook:

  • In BAS Explorer, right-click and select New File → name it test_genai.ipynb
  • Open the file — BAS will open it in the Jupyter editor
  • Click Select Kernel → Python Environments → select GenAI Hub (your venv)
  • If prompted to install ipykernel, confirm — it installs automatically

Step 5 — Verify the SDK connection in the first notebook cell:

# Verify SDK picks up credentials from .aicore-config.json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
 
client = OpenAI()
print('SDK connected successfully')

Do not commit .aicore-config.json to any Git repository. If you clone a repository into BAS and push back to Git, add .aicore-config.json to your .gitignore before the first commit. BAS has Git integration enabled by default — credential exposure via accidental commit is a real risk.

Once your dev space is running and the venv is activated, the SDK reads .aicore-config.json automatically. All five use case examples in this article run without any additional configuration changes from the BAS notebook environment.

Persisting the Virtual Environment Across BAS Sessions

BAS dev spaces suspend after inactivity. The virtual environment persists between sessions, but you must re-activate it each time you open a new terminal:

# Re-activate venv at the start of each BAS session

source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate

Tip: Add the activation command to your BAS dev space's shell profile (~/.bashrc) so it runs automatically when a new terminal opens: echo 'source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate' >> ~/.bashrc

Configuration: Local Python Script

  ▶  Environment: Local Python Script 

For local development, create a configuration file that points the SDK to your AI Core service key credentials. Store this as .aicore-config.json in your home directory or reference it via an environment variable.

environment variable.
# ~/.aicore-config.json — from your AI Core service key
{
  "AICORE_AUTH_URL": "https://<your-subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": "<clientid from service key>",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": "<clientsecret from service key>",
  "AICORE_BASE_URL": "<AI_API_URL from service key>",
  "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default"
}

Never commit .aicore-config.json to version control. Add it to your .gitignore immediately. For team environments, use environment variables (AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, etc.) instead of the config file — the SDK reads both patterns automatically.

Configuration: BTP Application

  ▶  Environment: BTP Application 

In BTP applications, bind the AI Core service instance to your application. The SDK reads credentials from the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable that BTP injects automatically at runtime — no manual credential management required.

# BTP injects VCAP_SERVICES automatically when AI Core is bound
# The SDK reads it without any additional configuration
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client
 
# SDK auto-resolves from VCAP_SERVICES in BTP runtime
proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

Once credentials are configured for your target environment, all five use case examples below work identically across all three environments. The SDK abstracts the credential source — your application code does not change.

FIVE SAP USE CASES WITH WORKING CODE 

SAP Data Tasks: Working Code Examples

The following examples cover five practical SAP use cases. Each uses the same SDK pattern — choose your preferred model by changing one parameter. All examples use Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the default; swap the model_name value for GPT-4o, Gemini, or Mistral as needed based on the model comparison in this week's LinkedIn post.

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Parse and summarize SAP SM21 / application error logs into structured root cause analysis

HANA query result analysis

Interpret query output and surface anomalies, trends, or data quality issues

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

Generate or explain code from natural language descriptions

Master data classification

Tag and categorize SAP master data records against defined classification schemes

Structured data extraction

Pull structured fields from SAP documents, change logs, or transport descriptions

Use Case 1: Summarizing SAP Exception Logs

SAP exception logs — SM21 system logs, application error dumps, job logs — are high-volume and time-consuming to triage manually. This pattern feeds raw log text to the model and returns a structured root cause summary with recommended actions.

# Use Case 1: SAP Exception Log Summarization
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
log_text = '''
  [SM21 Log Extract]
  E  RABAX  INCLUDE: ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD Line 847
  E  Runtime Error: COMPUTE_INT_PLUS_OVERFLOW
  E  Date: 2026-03-10  Time: 04:32:17
  E  Work process terminated abnormally
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis and ABAP expert.
  Analyze SAP exception logs and return:
  1. Root cause (one sentence)
  2. Affected component
  3. Severity: Critical / High / Medium / Low
  4. Recommended action
  Be concise and technical.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP log:\n{log_text}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Output example — Root cause: Integer overflow in delta load procedure at ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD line 847 due to 32-bit counter exceeding maximum value. Component: ABAP Runtime. Severity: Critical. Action: Cast counter variable to BIGINT and redeploy procedure.

Use Case 2: Analyzing HANA Query Results

This pattern connects to HANA Cloud, executes a query, and passes the result set to the model for interpretation — surfacing anomalies, trends, or data quality issues that would otherwise require manual review.

# Use Case 2: HANA Query Result Analysis
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import pandas as pd
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443,
    user='<user>',
    password='<password>',
    encrypt=True
)
 
# Run your query and load into DataFrame
query = '''
  SELECT PLANT, MATERIAL, SUM(QUANTITY) AS TOTAL_QTY,
         COUNT(*) AS RECORD_COUNT
  FROM STOCK_OVERVIEW
  GROUP BY PLANT, MATERIAL
  ORDER BY TOTAL_QTY DESC
  LIMIT 50
'''
df = pd.read_sql(query, conn)
 
# Pass result to model for analysis
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='You are an SAP data analyst. Identify anomalies, trends, and data quality issues in SAP query results. Be specific and reference actual values from the data.'),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP stock query result:\n{df.to_string()}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Do not pass full production datasets to the model. Apply LIMIT clauses and aggregate before sending. For sensitive SAP data, review your BTP data residency settings and confirm model data handling aligns with your organization's data governance policy before deploying to production.

Use Case 3: Generating ABAP / SQL / HANA Code from Natural Language

This pattern accepts a plain-language description of a requirement and returns production-ready ABAP, SQL, or HANA SQL code. Useful for accelerating development, explaining existing code, or onboarding team members to unfamiliar syntax.

# Use Case 3: SAP Code Generation from Natural Language
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
# GPT-4o recommended for ABAP — see model comparison article
 
requirement = '''
  Write a HANA SQL procedure that:
  - Reads from a virtual table SOURCE_SCHEMA.ORDERS
  - Filters records where CHANGE_DATE > last_run_date parameter
  - Inserts new records into TARGET_SCHEMA.ORDERS_DELTA
  - Updates a control table DELTA_CONTROL with the new last_run_date
  - Handles errors with a rollback
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL developer.
  Generate clean, production-ready HANA SQL code.
  Include comments explaining key sections.
  Flag any HANA-specific considerations or limitations.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Generate HANA SQL for this requirement:\n{requirement}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

 

Use Case 4: Classifying SAP Master Data

Master data classification — tagging materials, vendors, cost centers, or GL accounts against defined schemes — is high-volume and rule-heavy. This pattern uses the model to apply a classification schema to a batch of records and return structured JSON output ready for loading back into SAP.

# Use Case 4: SAP Master Data Classification
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
materials = [
  {'MATNR': '000000000010001234', 'MAKTX': 'Hydraulic Pump Assembly 250bar'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010005678', 'MAKTX': 'Office Chair Ergonomic Mesh Black'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010009012', 'MAKTX': 'SAP S4HANA License Annual Renewal'},
]
 
classification_schema = ['Mechanical Components', 'Office Supplies',
                         'Software & Licenses', 'Raw Materials', 'Services']
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify SAP material master records.
  Return ONLY valid JSON array. No explanation.
  Schema: {classification_schema}
  Format: [{{'MATNR': '...', 'CATEGORY': '...', 'CONFIDENCE': 'High/Medium/Low'}}]'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Classify these materials:\n{json.dumps(materials)}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
classified = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(classified, indent=2))

Prompt the model to return only JSON with no preamble or explanation when you need structured output for downstream processing. Claude is particularly consistent at following this instruction on classification tasks.

Use Case 5: Extracting Structured Data from SAP Documents

SAP landscapes accumulate semi-structured text in transport request descriptions, change log comments, functional spec attachments, and ticket notes. This pattern extracts structured fields from unstructured SAP document text — turning narrative descriptions into queryable data.

# Use Case 5: Structured Data Extraction from SAP Documents
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
transport_description = '''
  TR: DEVK912345 - FI Delta Load Fix
  Developer: firstname.lastname@company.com
  Changed ZFI_DELTA_LOAD procedure to fix integer overflow on
  BKPF document counter. Root cause was 32-bit INT hitting max
  value after 2.1B records. Cast to BIGINT. Tested on DEV and QA.
  Affected tables: BKPF, BSEG. No customizing changes.
  Approved by: firstname1.lastname1@company.com on 2026-03-08
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''Extract structured data from SAP transport descriptions.
  Return ONLY valid JSON. No explanation.
  Fields: transport_id, title, developer, approver, approval_date,
  affected_tables (list), root_cause, change_type, tested_systems (list)'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Extract fields from:\n{transport_description}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
extracted = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(extracted, indent=2))

Choosing the Right Model per Use Case

Based on practical testing across these five patterns — and detailed in this week's LinkedIn model comparison post — here are the recommended model defaults:

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — consistent on long, noisy log text

HANA query result analysis

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — structured output quality, handles wide DataFrames

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

GPT-4o — broader SAP code knowledge, stronger on ABAP specifics

Master data classification

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — reliable JSON-only output, consistent confidence scoring

Structured data extraction

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — long-form document coherence, clean JSON output

Swap model_name='gpt-4o' or model_name='mistral-large' in any example to compare outputs. The SDK pattern is identical across all models.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 3: Building Your First Prompt Pipeline — chaining prompts, managing context, structured outputs for multi-step SAP workflows
  • Part 4: Connecting to HANA Cloud Vector Store — embedding generation, REAL_VECTOR storage, RAG on your SAP data
  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI in your SAP extension layer
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by Generative AI Hub

If you work through any of these examples and run into environment-specific issues — particularly around BTP binding or AI Launchpad notebook credential resolution — drop a comment below. The configuration edge cases are where community knowledge adds the most value.

entry.title: Meet RPT-1 — SAP's Quiet Revolution in Enterprise AI
entry.content:
The Problem Nobody Talks About Enough

Every large enterprise sits on a mountain of structured data be it purchase orders(POs), invoices, payroll tables, supply chain logs. For decades, the promise was simple: feed this data into AI, get smarter decisions. But the reality was brutal.

Each prediction required a custom-trained model, months of data prep, and a team of data scientists — and still only solved one narrow problem. SAP looked at this bottleneck and built something different.

SAP RPT-1 Playground

 

Enter SAP RPT-1 — The "Rapid One"

Announced at SAP TechEd 2025, RPT-1 stands for Relational Pre-Trained Transformer, and SAP officially wants it pronounced "rapid one." It is not a chatbot or a text generator. It is a model built to understand and make predictions from relational data the structured transactional data that businesses actually run on. You show it a few solved example rows, and it immediately understands the patterns and delivers a prediction, and that too without weeks of model training or a single data scientist.

You can read about it more on SAP's website here https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html

You can also read the announcements made by @philipp_herzig in his announcements made on SAP Community and LinkedIn - https://shorto.in/3IpCg3

You don't have to believe me, try yourself. Here is again an investment from SAP for its customers and the world on the one and only FREE RPT playground - Free RPT Playground

Yes, you heard that right! It is a free playground which is accessible and covers sample scenario's/use cases, sample data, and a table showing predictions. 

I have personally tested from the point of view of Customer Success team. Maybe I will share the use case I am working on in a different blog. Keep a watch on my next blog or my LinkedIn announcement. 

Open Source

An open-source version, sap-rpt-1-oss, is available on Hugging Face and GitHub for research and non-commercial purposes

Real use cases across the enterprise

In Finance, an accountant can assign cost centers to invoices with just a few examples from last quarter — instantly.

In Supply Chain, a logistics manager can predict which shipments are most at risk during a disruption using real-time data.

In Sales, teams can identify the highest-conversion leads from a new campaign.

In HR, churn risk can be predicted from compensation history, performance scores, and tenure — enabling proactive interventions before talent walks out the door.

Instead of building and maintaining hundreds of separate models, businesses get a single, centrally managed foundation model embedded natively inside the SAP ecosystem.

Try It & Explore Further

  • SAP RPT-1 Official Page
  • Open Source on Hugging Face
  • SAP Community Deep Dive

Do not forget to read the terms on usage for the free playground on SAP website as below (below is an excerpt from www.sap.com)

The SAP-RPT playground is a demonstration platform. By using this service, you agree to comply with applicable data protection regulations. By uploading data to the SAP RPT playground, you acknowledge that you have the necessary rights and permissions to use this data. The data will be processed in accordance with our privacy policy and applicable data protection regulations. We do not store your data permanently, and it will be deleted after processing. For more information, please refer to our Terms of Service and Privacy Policy. Any given API token must be treated like a password. Never commit it to version control or share it in public forums.

Thank you

Feel free to share your views, learnings, findings and obviously feedback in the comment section below, which is crucial for all of us as customers, partners and SAP.

You can also reach out to me on LinkedIn. Here is link to my direct profile page https://shorto.in/aqB8qD

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 4 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  HANA CLOUD  |  RAG

SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store

End-to-end RAG pipeline on SAP data — REAL_VECTOR schema setup, SAP embedding model, document chunking, similarity search, and four working use cases: knowledge base search, data dictionary retrieval, transport log search, and master data context retrieval.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: HANA Cloud's native REAL_VECTOR data type turns your existing SAP database into a vector store — no separate infrastructure required. Combined with the SAP embedding model through AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, you get a fully BTP-native RAG pipeline on your SAP data. This article covers the complete path: schema setup, embedding generation, chunking strategy, similarity search, and four SAP-specific RAG use cases with working code.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–3 covered setup, Python SDK integration, and prompt pipelines. This part connects those pipelines to persistent SAP data through retrieval-augmented generation — the pattern that makes foundation model outputs grounded in your actual SAP context rather than general training data.

RAG is the architectural bridge between the prompt pipelines from Part 3 and the production SAP applications coming in Parts 5 and 6. Understanding it here makes the CAP and Joule integration articles that follow significantly easier to follow.

WHY RAG FOR SAP WORKLOADS 

Why Foundation Models Need RAG in SAP Contexts

Foundation models — even the most capable ones — have no knowledge of your SAP landscape. They do not know your custom Z-tables, your transport history, your material master conventions, your configured process variants, or the accumulated institutional knowledge in your SAP documentation. When you ask a foundation model an SAP question without context, it answers from general training data. That is not good enough for enterprise use.

RAG solves this by retrieving relevant context from your own SAP data at query time and passing it to the model alongside the question. The model's answer is grounded in your actual system — your data, your configuration, your history.

HANA Cloud makes this particularly clean for SAP practitioners because the vector store lives in the same database as your SAP data. There is no separate vector database to provision, secure, or maintain. Your embeddings sit alongside your operational data in HANA, with the same access controls, backup policies, and compliance posture you already manage.

SAP data sources → Chunk + embed → Store in HANA REAL_VECTOR → Query similarity search → Grounded model response

STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
