llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: New Learning Journey + Knowledge Badge: Joule & Business AI in SAP CX
entry.content:
On behalf of the Product learning team, I am excited to announce the launch of a new learning journey with a knowledge badge : 
Exploring Joule and Business AI in SAP Customer Experience.

This journey helps SAP CX professionals understand how Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, and SAP Business AI enhance productivity and transform customer experience processes across sales, service, commerce, and marketing. Learners will explore how AI-driven insights streamline decision-making, improve implementation efficiency, and support the evolving role of the next-generation CX consultant.  It ends in a self paced assessment, that helps obtain a knowledge badge.

🌟What This Learning Journey Covers

This learning journey provides a comprehensive introduction to:

  • The role of Joule and Business AI across SAP Customer Experience solutions  
  • How AI capabilities enhance CX business processes end‑to‑end  
  • Key Joule agent use cases supporting sales, service, commerce, and marketing scenarios  
  • How consultants can leverage Joule for faster solution design, configuration, troubleshooting, and advisory work  
  • How embedded AI accelerates implementations and supports the skill set of the Next Generation CX Consultant  

These insights equip learners to explore AI-enabled workflows and elevate their mastery of the SAP CX portfolio.

🎯Who Should Join?

This learning journey is ideal for:

  • SAP CX consultants
  • Solution architects
  • Business users and CX practitioners
  • Anyone preparing to work with AI-driven customer experience capabilities

🧠 How This Learning Journey Helps You

After completing the modules, learners will be able to:

  • Explain how Joule and Business AI enhance customer experience processes
  • Identify how Joule agents assist in real business scenarios
  • Understand how consultants can use AI to accelerate design, configuration, and optimization tasks
  • Navigate AI-driven productivity tools embedded across the SAP CX suite
     

🎓Earn a Knowledge Badge

Upon completing the learning journey, learners become eligible to attempt a Knowledge Badge assessment.

Here’s how it works:

  • You will answer 30 questions in a mix of multiple-choice and multiple-response formats.
  • The exam evaluates your understanding of Joule, Business AI concepts, and the scenarios covered in the learning journey.
  • Successful completion awards you an official SAP Knowledge Badge, which can be showcased on your SAP profile and shared externally.
  • You can attempt the questions any number of times.

This is a great way to validate your understanding and demonstrate your readiness for AI‑powered CX roles.

📚Start Learning Today

The learning journey is completely free and available now on SAP Learning.
Access it directly here:
https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/exploring-joule-and-business-ai-in-sap-customer-experience

entry.title: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content:
How Agentic AI Works Inside SAP Environments

The concept of Agentic AI refers to AI systems designed to operate with a defined objective while making structured decisions along the way. Instead of acting as a passive prediction engine, an agent evaluates context, selects actions, executes them, and learns from the outcome. When integrated with enterprise platforms, this model creates a layer of intelligent task execution across business processes. In SAP environments, this idea is gaining attention because enterprise workflows already contain structured data, defined process paths, and measurable outcomes.

The emergence of agentic AI in SAP builds upon the maturity of SAP data ecosystems. Platforms such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP Ariba, and SAP Integrated Business Planning generate high-quality operational data. When AI agents in SAP interact with these systems, they gain the ability to interpret transactional activity and act on it within defined operational boundaries.

For example, intelligent agents can operate across supply chain, finance, and logistics environments where large volumes of decisions are made every day.

Several real-world use cases demonstrate how AI agents interact with SAP workflows and support enterprise decision-making.

1. Autonomous Monitoring of Supply Disruptions

AI agents continuously review procurement signals, vendor commitments, and delivery schedules. When patterns indicate a disruption risk, the system flags the issue and proposes alternative sourcing actions before the disruption reaches operational teams. This early signal analysis helps organizations maintain supply continuity.

2. Transaction Validation Across Financial Workflows

Finance operations involve thousands of approvals, invoices, and transaction records. AI agents in SAP evaluate approval patterns, payment histories, and invoice structures. When irregularities appear, the system can pause a workflow or raise alerts for review. This structured monitoring helps finance teams maintain compliance discipline.

3. Operational Decision Support in Logistics Planning

Logistics operations depend on timely information about shipments, warehouse capacity, and transportation status. With AI for SAP, intelligent agents can interpret delays, route disruptions, or inventory shifts and recommend operational adjustments. These insights allow logistics teams to respond faster to operational signals.

These capabilities introduce a new operational layer for SAP AI teams. Rather than relying only on dashboards and reports, teams can deploy intelligent agents that act as operational co-workers. These agents will be continuously monitoring the system while executing pre-defined actions and escalating more complex operational decisions to human operators.

However, deploying Agentic for SAP Teams requires careful design. SAP environments consist of very mission-critical operations, and an incorrect decision could expose the organization to financial or regulatory risk. Organizations building SAP's AI frameworks will want to ensure that they have established some structured governance, testing protocols, and a defined controlled deployment pipeline prior to implementation.

Because of these complexities, many organizations are exploring specialized agentic AI services that help design, validate, and monitor agent-based architectures within SAP ecosystems.

Why SAP Teams Are Investing in Agentic AI Capabilities

The interest in agentic AI at SAP has increased rapidly because the capabilities of traditional automation have been reached by enterprise teams. Robotics Process Automation (RPA) and scripted workflows work well for processes with a predictable sequence. However, SAP systems are often used within a dynamic environment that is constantly changing. Market volatility, supply chain disruptions, regulatory changes, and inconsistent levels of demand can all result in needing systems to interpret the context of actions and respond appropriately.

Therefore, SAP AI agents provide an opportunity for companies to derive significant operational value. Instead of waiting for human input, intelligent agents interpret signals across multiple datasets and determine the next operational step within defined boundaries.

Several operational factors explain why SAP AI teams are actively evaluating agent-based architectures.

1. Decision Latency in Complex SAP Workflows

Enterprise SAP processes often involve multi-stage approvals and cross-system dependencies. When decisions depend on manual analysis, delays accumulate across operations.

With agentic for SAP teams, intelligent agents can:

  • Scan transactional records across modules
  • Identify decision triggers such as unusual pricing or delayed shipments
  • Initiate follow-up actions or escalation paths

This reduces operational friction while maintaining process oversight.

2. Continuous System Monitoring

Traditional monitoring systems alert teams only when predefined thresholds are crossed. However, SAP environments generate patterns that require contextual interpretation.

By applying AI for SAP, intelligent agents can review system activity continuously and detect early indicators of:

  • Financial inconsistencies
  • Procurement irregularities
  • Inventory allocation imbalances

Because AI agents in SAP evaluate historical patterns alongside real-time data, they can identify emerging risks before operational disruption occurs.

3. Intelligent Process Collaboration

Another reason SAP AI teams are studying agent-based architectures is the ability to coordinate activities across multiple systems. SAP environments often integrate with CRM platforms, logistics software, supplier networks, and external data providers.

Within such ecosystems, agentic AI in SAP can coordinate multiple operational steps:

  • Reviewing procurement demand forecasts
  • Triggering supplier communication workflows
  • Updating inventory planning models

This collaborative automation model enables agentic SAP teams to manage interconnected processes without continuous manual supervision.

4. Enterprise Governance and AI Accountability

Autonomous systems must operate under strict governance structures. SAP environments include compliance obligations, financial regulations, and audit requirements. Organizations, therefore, require transparency in how intelligent agents make decisions.

Well-designed agentic AI services address this concern by introducing:

  • Traceable decision logs
  • Controlled action permissions
  • Validation checkpoints before execution

These mechanisms help SAP AI teams maintain accountability while using AI for SAP in mission-critical environments.

As these operational drivers continue to grow, many enterprises are beginning to treat agent-based architectures as a strategic capability rather than an experimental technology initiative.

Conclusion

Agent-based intelligence represents a significant shift in enterprise automation. Instead of relying only on analytics dashboards or predictive models, organizations can deploy systems that interpret operational signals and take structured action. Within enterprise platforms, agentic AI in SAP allows intelligent agents to monitor workflows, identify decision triggers, and execute tasks under defined governance policies. As AI agents in SAP mature, they are gradually becoming an operational extension of human teams rather than a standalone technology layer.

For organizations exploring Agentic for SAP Teams, successful adoption depends on disciplined system validation, controlled deployment pipelines, and continuous monitoring. This is where specialized quality engineering becomes essential.

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 1 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between your first curl command and a working bearer token — what causes them, what they actually mean, and the exact fix for each one. Part 1 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Getting your first SAP AI Core bearer token working on Windows is harder than it should be. This article documents every authentication error between zero and a working token — PowerShell curl alias conflicts, quote character issues, port errors, missing OAuth path, empty responses, and RBAC denials — with the exact cause and fix for each. Part 2 covers deployment and configuration errors once authentication is working.

Why This Article Exists

The SAP AI Core documentation covers the happy path well. What it doesn't cover is the sequence of errors most practitioners hit on Windows before they ever get a working API call. These errors are not obscure — they come up consistently across different BTP setups, different teams, and different experience levels.

This article documents six authentication errors in the order they typically appear, based on real troubleshooting sessions. Each error has a clear cause and a specific fix. No generic advice — only what actually works.

Part 2 (Friday) covers what happens after authentication works: 404 on deployment calls, two-instance confusion, resource group provisioning failures, and orchestration vs foundation-models mismatches.

BEFORE YOU START 

What You Need

This guide assumes you have an SAP AI Core service instance on BTP with at least one service key created. From the service key JSON you need four values:

  • url — the OAuth authentication base URL (e.g. https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com)
  • clientid — your OAuth client ID
  • clientsecret — your OAuth client secret
  • serviceurls.AI_API_URL — your AI Core API base URL

The url field in your service key does NOT include /oauth/token. You must append it manually. This is the single most common cause of authentication failures and is covered in Error 5 below.

ERROR 1 OF 6  
  ERROR 1    PowerShell curl alias — -X not recognized    ParameterBindingException 
What You See
Invoke-WebRequest : A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name 'X'.
At line:1 char:6
+ curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token'
What Causes It

PowerShell has a built-in alias called curl that actually runs Invoke-WebRequest — Microsoft's own HTTP cmdlet. Invoke-WebRequest uses completely different syntax and does not understand the -X flag that real curl uses. When you type curl in PowerShell you are not running curl.exe — you are running Invoke-WebRequest.

The Fix

Two options — use Command Prompt instead of PowerShell, or explicitly call curl.exe to bypass the alias:

# Option 1 — Open Command Prompt (cmd) instead of PowerShell
# All curl commands in this guide use double quotes — required in cmd
 
# Option 2 — Stay in PowerShell but force the real curl binary
curl.exe -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

All remaining examples in this guide use Command Prompt syntax with double quotes. If you prefer PowerShell, add .exe after curl and the commands will work identically.

ERROR 2 OF 6  
  ERROR 2    Curly quotes / bad characters    curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

When you copy a curl command from a web page, Teams message, email, or PDF, the quote characters are often converted to typographic curly quotes (“ ”) instead of straight ASCII quotes (" "). Curly quotes look identical on screen but are invisible corruption to curl — it cannot parse the URL correctly and reports a nonsensical port error.

This error is almost always a quote character issue. The URL itself is correct.

The Fix

Do not paste the URL from a rich text source. Type the URL manually, or paste into a plain text editor (Notepad, VS Code) first to strip formatting, then copy from there into the terminal.

Also ensure all quote marks in your command are straight ASCII double quotes (") — not curly (“”) or single (‘’). In Command Prompt, single quotes are not valid string delimiters at all.

# Wrong — curly quotes from copy-paste (looks fine, breaks silently)
curl -X POST “https://...authentication.../oauth/token”
 
# Correct — straight ASCII double quotes
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token"
ERROR 3 OF 6  
  ERROR 3    Single quotes in Command Prompt    curl: (3) URL rejected 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

Single quotes work as string delimiters in PowerShell and Linux/Mac terminals. They do not work in Windows Command Prompt. If you copy a curl command that uses single quotes (common in documentation written for Linux) and run it in cmd, Command Prompt interprets the single quote as part of the URL string — causing the same port parsing error as curly quotes.

The Fix

Replace all single quotes with double quotes when running curl in Command Prompt:

# Wrong — single quotes (Linux/Mac syntax, fails in Windows cmd)
curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
 
# Correct — double quotes (Windows cmd syntax)
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded"

In Command Prompt, line continuation uses ^ (caret) not \ (backslash). If you copy Linux curl commands with \ at the end of each line, remove them and replace with ^ or put the entire command on one line.

ERROR 4 OF 6  
  ERROR 4    Empty response — no output at all    Command exits with no output 
What You See

The curl command runs, the prompt returns, but nothing is printed. No error, no response, no JSON — just silence.

What Causes It

Three common causes in order of likelihood:

  • The -d parameter contains an & character that Command Prompt interprets as a command separator — splitting your command into two separate commands, one of which runs silently
  • A missing closing quote somewhere in the command causes cmd to wait for more input silently
  • The response is very large and the terminal is suppressing it — redirect to a file to capture it
The Fix

The & in client_credentials&client_id=... must stay inside double quotes. If it's outside quotes, Command Prompt treats it as a command separator:

# Wrong — & outside quotes splits the command
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" -d grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=abc
 
# Correct — entire -d value inside double quotes
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

If you suspect a large response is being suppressed, redirect output to a file:

curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ... > output.txt

Open output.txt — the full response will be there regardless of terminal display limitations.

ERROR 5 OF 6  
  ERROR 5    401 Unauthorized — missing /oauth/token path    {"error":"unauthorized"} 
What You See
{"error":"unauthorized","error_description":"An Authentication object was not found in the SecurityContext"}
What Causes It

The url field in your AI Core service key JSON is the base authentication domain — it does not include the /oauth/token path. Many practitioners use this URL directly and hit 401 because they're calling the base domain rather than the token endpoint.

Compare these two:

# What's in your service key url field (base domain — not the token endpoint)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com
 
# What you need to call (token endpoint — append /oauth/token manually)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
The Fix

Always append /oauth/token to the url value from your service key. The SDK handles this automatically — but when calling directly with curl you must add it yourself:

curl -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful token call returns a JSON object containing access_token — a long base64-encoded string. This is your bearer token. Copy everything between the quotes after "access_token": and use it in all subsequent AI Core API calls. Tokens expire after 3600 seconds (1 hour) — generate a fresh one for each session.

ERROR 6 OF 6  
  ERROR 6    RBAC access denied on resource group creation    {"error":{"code":"403"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"403","message":"RBAC: access denied"}}
What Causes It

RBAC access denied on a resource group PUT call means your service key does not have admin scope for this AI Core instance. Service keys are created with specific role assignments — a key created without aicore_admin scope can generate tokens and list resources but cannot create or modify resource groups.

This is not a token expiry issue and not a URL issue. The token is working — the credentials behind it simply don't have the permission required.

The Fix

First — check if a resource group already exists before trying to create one. Many AI Core instances provisioned by BTP have a default resource group already:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If a resource group already exists with status PROVISIONED — use it. There is no need to create a new one.

If you genuinely need to create a new resource group and are hitting RBAC denial, recreate the service key with admin permissions:

  1. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → Service Keys
  2. Delete the existing service key
  3. Create a new service key — confirm aicore_admin role is included in the parameters
  4. Generate a new token using the new service key credentials

Check existing resource groups before attempting to create new ones. Most RBAC errors on resource group creation are unnecessary — the default resource group already exists and has status PROVISIONED. The GET call above takes 10 seconds and saves significant debugging time.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Authentication Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

ParameterBindingException — -X not recognized

PowerShell curl alias intercepting command

Use cmd or call curl.exe explicitly

Port number not decimal between 0 and 65535

Curly quotes from copy-paste corrupting URL

Type URL manually or paste through plain text editor first

Same port error with single quotes

Single quotes invalid in Windows cmd

Replace all single quotes with double quotes

No output — command exits silently

& in -d value treated as command separator by cmd

Keep entire -d value inside double quotes

401 — Authentication object not found

Calling base auth URL without /oauth/token suffix

Append /oauth/token to url value from service key

403 — RBAC access denied

Service key lacks aicore_admin scope

Check existing resource groups first — recreate key if creation is genuinely needed

THE WORKING COMMAND 

Complete Working Token Call for Windows Command Prompt

Once all six errors are resolved, this is the complete working token call. All values in capitals are replaced with your service key values:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful response looks like this — the access_token value is your bearer token:

{"access_token":"eyJhbGci...very long string...","token_type":"bearer","expires_in":3599}

Copy the full access_token value. In Part 2 we use it to call model deployments, diagnose 404 errors, resolve two-instance confusion, and fix resource group provisioning failures.

If your response contains access_token — authentication is complete. Every error in this article is behind you. Part 2 covers everything from this point to a working model response.

entry.title: The AI Revolution in CX: How SAP Business AI is Redefining the Customer Journey
entry.content:
Today’s customers demand intelligent, personalized experiences. To deliver, businesses need more than just good intentions—they need powerful, embedded intelligence. SAP is delivering this with SAP Business AI, a comprehensive strategy that infuses the entire Customer Experience (CX) suite with practical, powerful artificial intelligence, fronted by its conversational copilot, Joule.

This isn't just about AI for your customers; it's about AI for your entire organization.

 AI for Your Frontline Teams: Smarter Engagement

SAP Business AI works out-of-the-box to make your daily operations more intelligent. With Joule as their copilot, your teams can work faster and smarter.

  • For Sales: Joule drafts personalized emails and summarizes opportunities, while embedded AI scores leads so reps can focus on the deals most likely to close.
  • For Service: Joule instantly summarizes complex customer tickets, and AI recommends the best knowledge base solutions, slashing resolution times and boosting satisfaction.
  • For Commerce: AI-powered search and product recommendations create a bespoke shopping experience for every single visitor, driving conversion and loyalty.

AI for Your Project Teams: Faster Implementations

SAP’s AI vision goes deeper, empowering the people who build your CX solutions with Joule for Consultants (J4C).

Think of J4C as an expert "AI librarian" for your project team. It’s a specialized copilot that provides instant, conversational access to SAP's vast knowledge base. Consultants can ask for best practices, configuration guidance, and even code intelligence. By mastering smart prompting, teams can dramatically reduce project complexity and accelerate delivery with confidence.

Ready to Master SAP Business AI?

Dive deep by enrolling in the official SAP Learning course and earning your Badge!

Introducing SAP Business AI for SAP Customer Experience

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Deployment and Configuration Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 2 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 2 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Deployment and Configuration Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between a working bearer token and a working model response — 404 on inference calls, two AI Core instance confusion, orchestration vs foundation-models mismatch, resource group provisioning failures, and 409 on delete. Part 2 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 1 covered getting your bearer token working. This part covers everything after that — why your inference call returns 404 even though the deployment shows RUNNING, how two AI Core instances cause invisible failures, why the orchestration scenario requires a different request format, and what to do when resource group creation fails with 500 or gets stuck in Provisioning. These errors are harder to diagnose because the symptoms are misleading and the causes are architectural, not syntactic.

Starting Point for This Article

This article assumes Part 1 is complete — you have a working bearer token from a successful OAuth call. The errors here all occur after authentication is working. If you're still hitting 401 or token issues, start with Part 1.

The six errors in this article follow the same sequence most practitioners hit: successfully authenticated, deployment appears to exist, but inference calls fail. Each error reveals a layer of the AI Core architecture that isn't obvious from the documentation.

ERROR 7 OF 12  
ERROR 7    404 on inference call — deployment exists in Launchpad    {"error":{"code":"404"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"404","message":"Resource not found"}}

This happens even though you can see the deployment in AI Launchpad with status RUNNING.

What Causes It

Three common causes — all produce the same 404:

  • The deployment belongs to a different AI Core instance than the one your service key authenticates against (covered in detail in Error 😎
  • The token expired between the deployments list call and the inference call — tokens are valid for 3600 seconds (1 hour)
  • The AI-Resource-Group header is missing or pointing to the wrong resource group
The Fix

First — confirm the deployment ID matches exactly. Get a fresh list with a fresh token:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/lm/deployments" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_FRESH_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default"

Find your deployment in the response. Confirm the id field matches the ID in your inference call URL exactly. Then run the inference call immediately with the same fresh token:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_FRESH_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}],"max_tokens":100}"

If your deployments list returns the ID but the inference call still returns 404 — the deployment belongs to a different AI Core instance. This is Error 8.

ERROR 8 OF 12  

ERROR 8    Two AI Core instances — wrong service key    404 on RUNNING deployment 
What You See

Your deployment shows RUNNING in AI Launchpad. Your token call succeeds. Your deployments list curl returns results. But the inference call returns 404 — consistently, with every fresh token.

What Causes It

Many SAP BTP accounts have multiple AI Core service instances — created at different times for different teams or projects. AI Launchpad can connect to multiple instances and lets you switch between them using the workspace selector in the header.

The critical issue: your service key belongs to one AI Core instance. Your deployment was created using a different AI Core instance in Launchpad. Your token authenticates successfully against your instance — but that instance has no record of the deployment that lives in the other instance. The result is a 404 that looks identical to a missing deployment.

How to Diagnose It

Three checks that identify this issue in under two minutes:

  1. Check your service key — the AI_API_URL field identifies which instance your credentials belong to
  2. In AI Launchpad — check the workspace header. It shows which AI Core connection is currently active. Note the name.
  3. Check the resource group labels — in AI Launchpad → Resource Groups → edit the default group → look at the instance_id label. This shows which AI Core instance owns the resource group.

If the instance_id in the resource group labels does not match the instance your service key belongs to — you have the two-instance problem.

The Fix

Two options depending on your access:

  • Option 1 (fastest): Get a service key for the AI Core instance that owns the resource group containing your deployment. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → find the correct instance → Service Keys → Create. Use the new credentials for token generation and all API calls.
  • Option 2 (self-contained): Create a new resource group in your own AI Core instance, create a new configuration and deployment there, and use your existing service key throughout. This requires your service plan to support additional resource group creation — see Error 9 if this fails.

The instance_id label in the resource group is the fastest way to identify this issue. It directly tells you which AI Core instance the resource group belongs to — compare it against the instance identifier in your service key URL.

ERROR 9 OF 12  
ERROR 9    Resource group stuck in Provisioning    500 on creation / status never changes 
What You See

You create a new resource group via AI Launchpad or curl PUT. The creation returns a 500 error or appears to succeed but the status shows Provisioning and never changes — even after 10+ minutes.

What Causes It

Two common causes:

  • Your AI Core service plan does not support creating additional resource groups. Some SAP internal plans and certain BTP subscription tiers restrict resource group creation to the default group only.
  • The AI Core instance itself has an underlying provisioning issue — a platform-level problem that requires SAP support to resolve.
The Fix

Step 1 — Wait 5 minutes before concluding provisioning has failed. New resource groups typically provision in under 5 minutes. A 10+ minute wait with no status change indicates a failure.

Step 2 — Verify your service plan supports additional resource groups. Check BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → plan details. If the plan explicitly restricts resource groups, creating new ones is not possible without upgrading.

Step 3 — If the resource group is stuck, delete it and recreate via curl (more reliable than the UI for initial creation):

# Delete stuck resource group
curl -X DELETE "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/YOUR_GROUP_ID" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"
 
# Recreate via curl
curl -X PUT "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"resourceGroupId": "your-new-group"}"

Step 4 — Check status after 3 minutes:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/your-new-group" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If status remains Provisioning after recreation — raise a support ticket with component CA-ML-AIP, include your instance ID and the timestamp of the failed creation attempt.

ERROR 10 OF 12  
ERROR 10    409 on resource group delete    {"error":{"code":"409"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"409","message":"Conflict"}}

This occurs when attempting to delete a resource group that is still in Provisioning state.

What Causes It

HTTP 409 Conflict means the resource is in a state that doesn't allow the requested operation. A resource group in Provisioning state is actively being worked on by the platform — deleting it while provisioning is in progress is not permitted.

The Fix

Wait for the provisioning process to either complete (status: PROVISIONED) or fail (status: ERROR) before attempting to delete. If the status has been Provisioning for over 15 minutes without changing, the platform may be unresponsive — in this case wait 30 minutes before retrying the delete.

If the delete still fails after the status shows ERROR, use the curl DELETE command rather than the UI — the API is more reliable for deleting failed resource groups:

curl -X DELETE "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/YOUR_GROUP_ID" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"
ERROR 11 OF 12  
ERROR 11    Orchestration vs foundation-models scenario mismatch    Empty response or wrong endpoint format 
What You See

Your deployments list returns a deployment with status RUNNING, but when you call the inference endpoint using the standard chat/completions format, you get an empty response or a format error.

What Causes It

SAP AI Core has two distinct deployment scenarios — foundation-models and orchestration. They use different inference endpoint formats and different request body structures.

  • foundation-models deployments use /chat/completions with a standard OpenAI-compatible request body including a model field
  • orchestration deployments use /completion with an orchestration_config wrapper structure — a completely different format

If your deployment was created under the orchestration scenario (check the scenarioId field in the deployments list response) but you're calling it with the foundation-models format, the request either fails silently or returns a format error.

How to Check Your Scenario

In your deployments list response, find the scenarioId field for your deployment:

{"id":"d01e9590...","scenarioId":"orchestration",...}  ← orchestration deployment
{"id":"df600c1a...","scenarioId":"foundation-models",...}  ← foundation-models deployment
The Fix — foundation-models deployment (standard format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}],"max_tokens":100}"
The Fix — orchestration deployment (orchestration format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/completion" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"orchestration_config":{"module_configurations":{"llm_module_config":{"model_name":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","model_params":{"max_tokens":500}}}},"messages_history":[],"template":{"template":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}}"

For most use cases — direct model access for Python applications and RAG pipelines — the foundation-models scenario is the correct choice. Orchestration is for SAP's managed prompt orchestration service with additional capabilities like content filtering and grounding. If you want direct foundation model access, create your configuration with scenarioId: foundation-models.

ERROR 12 OF 12  
ERROR 12    AI Launchpad showing wrong instance    Configurations visible but inference fails 
What You See

You create a configuration and deployment in AI Launchpad and can see it listed. But when you use curl with your service key credentials, the deployment is either not found in the list or returns 404 on inference.

What Causes It

AI Launchpad is a separate BTP subscription that can connect to multiple AI Core instances. The workspace selector in the Launchpad header controls which instance you're viewing and managing. If you have subscriptions to AI Launchpad in multiple BTP subaccounts, each may open to a different AI Core connection by default.

The result: you create a deployment while connected to instance A in Launchpad, but your service key belongs to instance B. The Launchpad URL in the browser contains the subaccount subdomain — this is the clearest indicator of which Launchpad instance you're using.

How to Diagnose It

Check the browser URL when you're in AI Launchpad. The subdomain in the URL identifies which BTP subaccount (and therefore which AI Launchpad subscription) you're using:

https://YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.ai-launchpad.prod.us-east-1.aws.ai-prod.cloud.sap/...

Compare YOUR-SUBDOMAIN against the subdomain in your service key's url field. If they don't match — you're in the wrong Launchpad.

The Fix
  1. Go to BTP Cockpit → the subaccount that contains your AI Core service instance
  2. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Launchpad → Open
  3. This opens the Launchpad connected to that specific subaccount's AI Core instance
  4. Create your configuration and deployment here — it will be visible to your service key credentials

Bookmark the AI Launchpad URL for each subaccount separately. When you have multiple subaccounts, it's easy to open the wrong Launchpad instance by navigating directly to the URL without checking which subaccount it belongs to.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Deployment and Configuration Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

404 on RUNNING deployment

Token expired or wrong resource group header

Refresh token and confirm AI-Resource-Group header matches deployment's group

404 despite correct token and group

Deployment lives in different AI Core instance than service key

Check instance_id label in resource group — get service key for the correct instance

Resource group stuck in Provisioning

Plan restriction or platform-level issue

Wait 5 min, retry via curl, raise CA-ML-AIP ticket if persists beyond 15 min

409 on resource group delete

Resource group still in active Provisioning state

Wait for PROVISIONED or ERROR status before deleting

Empty response or format error on inference

Calling orchestration deployment with foundation-models format

Check scenarioId in deployment list — use /completion endpoint for orchestration

Deployment in Launchpad but not in curl list

Launchpad connected to wrong subaccount's AI Core instance

Open Launchpad from correct BTP subaccount — check browser URL subdomain

THE WORKING INFERENCE CALL 

Complete Working Model Inference Call

Once all twelve errors are resolved, this is the complete working inference call for a foundation-models deployment. All values in capitals are replaced with your actual values:

# Step 1: Get fresh token (valid 1 hour)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"
 
# Step 2: List deployments to confirm ID and resource group
curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/lm/deployments" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default"
 
# Step 3: Call the model
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"YOUR_MODEL_NAME","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello, are you working?"}],"max_tokens":100}"

A successful response contains choices[0].message.content with the model's reply. Your end-to-end connection is proven. From here — move to the generative-ai-hub-sdk for Python integration (covered in the Generative AI Hub series) which handles token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically.

entry.title: Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is almost here, and SAP Build at Sapphire is where customers and partners will be able to see how to easily create and deploy AI agents starting with simple language descriptions.  

For anyone tracking where AI agents and agentic development are headed in the enterprise, SAP Build will offer a chance to see it up close. Attendees will be able to experience how SAP Build helps teams build AI agents across SAP. This includes hands-on experience, live demos, sessions, and direct access to the teams building these capabilities. 

This year, the focus is on making agentic development real - moving from intent to working solution through a guided, end-to-end experience. 

 The hands-on experience: Build an agent with Joule Studio 

The biggest draw for SAP Build at Sapphire will be the hands-on experience on the show floor. 

Attendees can sit down at an open station and work through guided scenarios in a live system – building agents with Joule Studio, generating app logic from intent, and integrating with SAP data and workflows. This is a practical view at how agentic development works from start to deployment. 

This experience builds on the popular SAP Build Developer Garage at TechEd, which became a go-to stop for developers who wanted to get hands-on and see SAP Build in action. At Sapphire, we are bringing that energy back and making it bigger for a broader audience. 

Space will be limited, and access will be first-come, first-serve, so this is one stop worth making early. 

The sessions 

SAP Build sessions at Sapphire will focus on how teams are building AI agents, clean-core extensions and automation workflows. You’ll see how agentic development is evolving - from defining intent to generating and deploying working solutions - and how Joule is integrated across the development lifecycle. 

For anyone trying to separate AI hype from AI execution, these sessions are worth prioritizing. 

The booth 

The SAP Build and Agentic AI booths are where live walk-throughs and real conversations happen. 

Bring your own use cases, connect directly with SAP experts, and validate how SAP Build fits into your architecture. Whether you are building your first extension or scaling AI-driven workflows, the booth’s are designed for both strategic and practical discussions.  

Expect demos, conversations, and a chance to get closer to what SAP Build is showing at Sapphire. 

 

Why stop by SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 

AI agents are a major focus at Sapphire but SAP Build is where you can see how they are actually built and deployed in SAP environments. 

Get hands-on. Talk to the experts. See how agentic development works in practice. 

Plan your SAP Build stop at SAP Sapphire: 

  • Register for SAP Sapphire 
  • Browse SAP Build sessions 
  • Find the SAP Build booth (see the map onsite)

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the RPT-1 API Token
entry.content:
In my previous blog about experimenting with RPT-1, we explored how to try out using the RPT-1 playground version. Now, let’s see how this can be done programmatically. Please check the  SAP Cloud SDK for Python for RPT-1 native APIs. We will first use request library to call the API directly.

Prerequisites:

  1. An API key for the service – you can get it from the ‘Your API Token’ section at https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs.

 

 

 

We will use the same .csv file used previously for the Freight Orders with ‘[PREDICT]’ tag.

RPT-1 API accept json format. So, lets first convert .csv file to json format.

#Read csv and convert to json
import csv
import json
def csv_to_json(csv_file_path):

    data = []
    with open(csv_file_path, encoding='utf-8') as csv_file:
       reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file) 
        for row in reader:
            data.append(row
    final_payload = {
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": "Carrier",
                    "prediction_placeholder": "[PREDICT]"
                }
            ]
        },
        "index_column": "Document" ,
        "rows": data         
    }
    print(f"Done! {len(data)} records converted to json")
    return final_payload

 

Prepare the request parameters

access_token = "<<Your access token here>>" #Use .env for secure storage
url = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"

Now call the API

import requests
import json
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}
final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv')
response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=final_payload, timeout=120)
response.raise_for_status()
data_pred = response.json()
preds = data_pred.get("prediction", [])
print(json.dumps(preds, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

The result would look like this.

{
  "id": "ba15b9bd-8cde-4eee-b858-0329f1b48e11",
  "metadata": {
    "num_columns": 38,
    "num_predictions": 1,
    "num_query_rows": 1,
    "num_rows": 334
  },
  "predictions": [
    {
      "Carrier": [
        {
          "confidence": 0.46,
          "confidence_interval": null,
          "prediction": "MM_CAR_01"
        }
      ],
      "Document": 6100000600
    }
  ],
  "status": {
    "code": 0,
    "message": "ok"
  }
}

If you have RPT-1 deployment in SAP AI Launchpad, then you can use native API call.

import os
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.models import RPTRequest, PredictionConfig, TargetColumn
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.client import RPTClient
#Get the credentials from the AI Core instance in your BTP sub-account
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "<<AICORE_AUTH_URL>>oauth/token"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_ID>>"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET>>"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "<<AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP>>"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "<<AICORE_BASE_URL>>/v2"

final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv', target_columns=["Carrier"])

client = RPTClient()
body = RPTRequest(
            prediction_config=PredictionConfig(
                target_columns=[
                    TargetColumn(name="Carrier", task_type="classification")
                ]),
            rows=final_payload
        )
response = client.predict(body=body, model_name="sap-rpt-1-small")
print(response.predictions)

This example shows using Python, but this can be done in ABAP as well using HTTP request call (such as a save process controller strategy method in SAP TM). 

What you learned

  • How to shape tabular data for SAP RPT‑1 with a clear prediction_config and index_column
  • How to invoke the predict API(using request call and native API) securely and read confidence‑scored outputs
  • We used 'predicting carrier' just as an example and to understand the usage. You can think of any other use cases which require prediction from minimal data set which doesn't require complex classic ML training.

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 Meets Employee Attrition: A Hands-On Test with SuccessFactors Data
entry.content:
The Short Version

I wanted to see if SAP’s new tabular AI model, RPT-1, could predict which employees are likely to quit, using nothing but a CSV extracted from SuccessFactors fields. No model training. No data science team. Just upload and predict.

To keep things honest, I based the experiment on a real peer-reviewed study that used actual employee data and traditional ML. The result? RPT-1 nailed all 5 test predictions in under 400 milliseconds. But the full story is more interesting than the headline.

 

Why I Picked Attrition (and Not Another Sales Demo)

Credit where it's due: Hari Panakkal's recent SAP Community post, Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground, is a great walkthrough of RPT-1 on transportation data. It got me thinking: if RPT-1 works that well for carrier prediction in TM, what happens when you point it at an HR problem?

Most RPT-1 demos I've seen focus on sales forecasting or logistics. Those are solid use cases, but the one I care about is people analytics, and the single biggest question HR teams ask is: "Who's about to leave?"

Attrition is a natural fit for RPT-1 because:

  • The data is tabular and already sitting in SuccessFactors
  • The problem is binary classification (stays vs. leaves)
  • Most HR teams don't have ML engineers on staff

But I didn't want to just throw random HR data at it. I wanted to test RPT-1 against something with a known answer.

 

The Research Behind the Experiment

I grounded everything in this study:

Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.

Here’s what makes this paper worth paying attention to:

It used real data. Not overused Kaggle dataset, but actual records from an Italian financial company. 5,767 employees. 309 voluntary resignations (a 5.4% attrition rate, which is realistic and makes the problem genuinely hard).

They tested four algorithms head-to-head. Random Forest crushed everything: 99.9% AUC, 94.4% sensitivity. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees weren’t even close.

They found things that don’t match your intuition. This is the best part. Using SHAP analysis, they discovered that employees who received bonuses were more likely to leave (burnout from overperforming), and employees who got retention incentives also showed higher attrition. It turns out retention incentives are targeted at people already flagged as flight risks, so the incentive is a signal of existing risk, not a cause of it.

The ML pipeline was serious work. ROSE resampling for class imbalance, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP post-processing. This took significant data science effort.

So the question became: Can RPT-1 get comparable results with a single API call?

 

Mapping the Variables to SuccessFactors

The study used 14 employee attributes. I mapped each one to the actual OData entity and field in SuccessFactors, and validated every mapping against the live API:

 

Research Variable

Where It Lives in SF

Category

Gender

PerPersonal.gender

Personal

Age Class

Derived from PerPerson.dateOfBirth

Personal

Tenure Class

Derived from EmpEmployment.startDate

People

Department

EmpJob.department

Work Environment

Rank

EmpJob.payGrade → FOPayGrade (grouped)

Management

Contract Type

EmpJob.contractType

Management

Job Level

EmpJob.payGrade

Management

Talent Program

TalentPool membership

People

Bonus Received

EmpPayCompNonRecurring existence

Management

Hours (PT/FT)

EmpJob.standardHours

Management

Retention Incentive

Custom MDF or EmpPayCompRecurring

Management

Job Family

FOJobCode.jobFunction

Management

Log Salary

EmpPayCompRecurring.paycompvalue (log10)

Management

Log Premium

Performance bonus (log10)

Management

Picture 1: Ishikawa – Fishbone diagram

 

What Is RPT-1, Exactly?

SAP RPT-1 is a Relational Pretrained Transformer , a foundation model built for tabular data, not text. Announced at TechEd 2025, it comes in three flavors:

  • sap-rpt-1-small : Fast commercial model (low latency)
  • sap-rpt-1-large : Higher accuracy commercial model
  • sap-rpt-1-oss : Open-source on GitHub (Apache 2.0)

The key idea: you give it labeled rows as examples and rows with [PREDICT] placeholders as questions. It learns from the context: no training, no feature engineering, no resampling. Think of it like few-shot prompting in ChatGPT, but for spreadsheets.

 

Running the Experiment

Step 1: Build the Dataset

I created sf_employee_attrition.csv with 50 labeled employee records using the 14 features from the research, plus 5 query rows with [PREDICT] in the ATTRITION column. The labeled data reflects the patterns the study found: higher attrition among younger, shorter-tenure employees with lower compensation, and the counterintuitive bonus/retention effects.

 

Picture 2 - sf_employee_attrition.csv, 50 labeled + 5 query rows, 14 features mapped to SF fields

 

Step 2: The No-Code Route (Playground)

Easiest way to test. Go to rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard:

  1. Upload the CSV
  2. The interface detects the [PREDICT] cells
  3. Click Predict
  4. Done

No coding. Supports up to 2,073 rows and 50 columns.

 

Step 3: The API Route (for Pipelines)

This option allows full control and evaluation metrics:

"""
SAP RPT-1 Employee Attrition Prediction
========================================
This script demonstrates how to use SAP-RPT-1 (Relational Pretrained Transformer)
to predict employee attrition using structured HR data modeled after
SuccessFactors workforce analytics dimensions.

Dataset design informed by: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025),
"Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants",
Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.


Prerequisites:
  - For API approach: SAP RPT Playground account (https://rpt.cloud.sap)
  - For OSS approach: Python 3.11+, GPU with 24-80GB VRAM recommended

Author: Shakti Barath
"""

import json
import csv
import requests
import os
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================

# Option A: Load token from file (recommended)
# Create access_token.json with: {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}
# Get your token from: https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs (scroll to bottom)
TOKEN_FILE = "access_token.json"

# Option B: Set as environment variable
# export SAP_RPT_TOKEN="your_token_here"

RPT_API_URL = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"
CSV_FILE = "sf_employee_attrition.csv"
TARGET_COLUMN = "ATTRITION"
PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER = "[PREDICT]"

def load_token():
    """Load API token from file or environment variable."""
    if os.path.exists(TOKEN_FILE):
        with open(TOKEN_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get("token", "")
    return os.environ.get("SAP_RPT_TOKEN", "")

def load_csv_as_rows(filepath):
    """
    Load CSV and convert to RPT-1 row format.
    
    RPT-1 expects rows as a list of dicts. Rows with [PREDICT] in the 
    target column serve as query rows; all others are context rows
    (few-shot examples for in-context learning).
    """
    rows = []
    with open(filepath, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            rows.append(dict(row))
    return rows

def build_payload(rows, target_column, index_column="EMP_ID"):
    """
    Build the RPT-1 API request payload.
    
    Structure:
    - rows: list of dicts (context + query rows)
    - index_column: unique identifier column
    - prediction_config: specifies target and task type
    
    Query rows contain [PREDICT] in the target column.
    Context rows contain actual labels — these are the few-shot examples.
    """
    payload = {
        "rows": rows,
        "index_column": index_column,
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": target_column,
                    "placeholder_value": PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER,
                    "task_type": "classification"
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    return payload

def call_rpt1_api(payload, token):
    """Send prediction request to SAP RPT-1 Playground API."""
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json"
    }
    
    print("Sending prediction request to SAP RPT-1...")
    print(f"  Context rows: {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    print(f"  Query rows:   {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) == PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    
    response = requests.post(RPT_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=120)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

def display_predictions(result, rows):
    """Display RPT-1 predictions alongside employee context."""
    predictions = result.get("prediction", {}).get("predictions", [])
    
    # Build lookup for employee context
    emp_lookup = {str(r["EMP_ID"]): r for r in rows}
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1 ATTRITION PREDICTIONS")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    for pred in predictions:
        emp_id = str(pred.get("id", ""))
        emp = emp_lookup.get(emp_id, {})
        attrition_pred = pred.get(TARGET_COLUMN, [{}])
        
        if isinstance(attrition_pred, list) and len(attrition_pred) > 0:
            predicted_value = attrition_pred[0].get("prediction", "N/A")
            confidence = attrition_pred[0].get("confidence")
        else:
            predicted_value = "N/A"
            confidence = None
        
        risk_indicator = "HIGH RISK" if predicted_value == "Yes" else "LOW RISK"
        
        print(f"\n  Employee {emp_id}: {emp.get('JOB_FAMILY', 'Unknown')}")
        print(f"  Department: {emp.get('DEPARTMENT', '-')} | "
              f"Age: {emp.get('AGE_CLASS', '-')} | "
              f"Tenure: {emp.get('TENURE_CLASS', '-')}")
        print(f"  Talent Program: {emp.get('TALENT_PROGRAM', '-')} | "
              f"Contract: {emp.get('CONTRACT_TYPE', '-')}")
        print(f"  Prediction: {predicted_value} ({risk_indicator})")
        if confidence is not None:
            print(f"  Confidence: {confidence:.2%}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)

def analyze_context_patterns(rows):
    """
    Analyze attrition patterns in context data.
    Mirrors the descriptive statistics approach from the research paper
    (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025, Table 6).
    """
    context_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER]
    
    print("\n" + "-" * 60)
    print("CONTEXT DATA ANALYSIS (mirrors research methodology)")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    # Attrition by age class
    age_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        age = r["AGE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if age not in age_stats:
            age_stats[age] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        age_stats[age]["total"] += 1
        age_stats[age]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Age Class:")
    for age in sorted(age_stats.keys()):
        s = age_stats[age]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {age:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Attrition by tenure class
    tenure_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        tenure = r["TENURE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if tenure not in tenure_stats:
            tenure_stats[tenure] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        tenure_stats[tenure]["total"] += 1
        tenure_stats[tenure]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Tenure Class:")
    for tenure in sorted(tenure_stats.keys()):
        s = tenure_stats[tenure]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {tenure:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Key insight from the research
    print("\n  Key Insight (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025):")
    print("  Younger employees and those with shorter tenure show")
    print("  significantly higher attrition rates. The research found")
    print("  15.9% attrition for age 18-25 vs 1.4% for age 50-65.")

# ============================================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1: Employee Attrition Prediction Experiment")
    print("Grounded in: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025), ESWA 272")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # Load data
    if not Path(CSV_FILE).exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {CSV_FILE} not found. Place it in the same directory.")
        return
    
    rows = load_csv_as_rows(CSV_FILE)
    print(f"\nLoaded {len(rows)} employee records from {CSV_FILE}")
    
    # Analyze context patterns
    analyze_context_patterns(rows)
    
    # Load token
    token = load_token()
    if not token:
        print("\n" + "-" * 60)
        print("NO API TOKEN FOUND")
        print("-" * 60)
        print("To run predictions with the RPT Playground API:")
        print("1. Sign up at https://rpt.cloud.sap")
        print("2. Copy your token from https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs")
        print("3. Save it to access_token.json:")
        print('   {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}')
        print("\nAlternatively, use the OSS version (see rpt1_oss_predict.py)")
        return
    
    # Build payload and predict
    payload = build_payload(rows, TARGET_COLUMN)
    
    try:
        result = call_rpt1_api(payload, token)
        display_predictions(result, rows)
        
        # Save raw response
        with open("rpt1_predictions.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nRaw predictions saved to rpt1_predictions.json")
        
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f"\nAPI Error: {e}")
        print("Check your token and ensure the playground is accessible.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Create access_token.json in the same folder with your RPT-1 API key:

{
  "token": "YOUR_RPT-API_KEY"
}

 

The Results

I ran the CSV through the RPT Playground API. Here’s exactly what came back:

EMP ID

Profile

Prediction

Confidence

Why It Makes Sense

1051

Finance • Staff • Permanent

Yes

95%

Age 25–30, Tenure 0–3 yr, Talent Program, Low premium

1052

IT • Staff • Permanent

No

98%

Age 30–40, Tenure 10–20 yr, Bonus received, Strong salary

1053

Sales • Staff • Fixed-Term

Yes

99%

Age 18–25, Tenure 0–3 yr, Fixed-term, Lowest salary

1054

HR • Manager • Permanent

No

99%

Age 40–50, Tenure 10–20 yr, Manager rank, High comp

1055

Ops • Staff • Permanent

Yes

94%

Age 25–30, Talent Prog, Retention incentive, Lower salary

 

Accuracy

Avg Confidence

Inference Time

Context Rows

5/5 (100%)

97%

399 ms

50 labeled rows

 

What Stood Out

The easy calls were easy. Employee 1053 (young, short-tenure, fixed-term, lowest salary) and Employee 1054 (senior manager, long tenure, high pay) both got 99% confidence. These are the extremes, exactly where the research paper’s Random Forest also showed the clearest separation.

Employee 1055 was the interesting one. This person has moderate tenure (3–10 years), which in the training data strongly correlates with staying. But RPT-1 correctly flagged them as at-risk at 94% confidence. Why? The combination of a retention incentive, talent program membership, and lower compensation. Remember what the research found: retention incentives are a signal that someone is already a flight risk, not a shield against it. RPT-1 picked up that interaction from just 50 context rows. That’s impressive.

399 milliseconds. The research paper required ROSE resampling, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning across four algorithms, and SHAP post-processing. RPT-1 got directionally equivalent predictions with a single API call on raw CSV data. For an HR team that wants a quick risk screening before committing to a full ML build, that’s a big deal.

The honest caveat: This is a 50-row synthetic dataset with intentionally clear patterns. Production data with 5,767 employees and a 5.4% attrition rate would be a harder test. These results show RPT-1 can read tabular patterns well. A head-to-head on real production data is the next step.

 

What RPT-1 Is Good At Here

  • No ML pipeline required. The research team spent serious effort on resampling, cross-validation, and algorithm selection. RPT-1 skips all of that.
  • Handles mixed data out of the box. Categorical and numerical fields go in as-is. No encoding needed.
  • Tolerates missing data. The research had to drop 5 variables due to gaps. RPT-1 handles missing values by design.
  • Easy to iterate. Want to add engagement survey scores? Just add a column. No pipeline rebuild.

 

What RPT-1 Can’t Do (Yet)

  • No explainability. RPT-1 gives you a prediction and a confidence score, but not why. For HR decisions, that matters a lot.
  • Class imbalance is your problem. With a 5.4% attrition rate, your context rows need careful curation.
  • No time-series awareness. RPT-1 works on static snapshots. It can’t predict when someone will leave.
  • Playground has limits. 2,073 rows max. For production scale, deploy via SAP AI Core.

 

So What Should You Actually Do With This?

RPT-1 makes it trivially easy to generate attrition predictions from SuccessFactors data. But predictions without understanding can lead to bad interventions. (Remember the retention incentive example: a naïve reading suggests incentives cause attrition.)

My recommendation:

  1. Use RPT-1 as a rapid screening tool. Upload your SF extract, get a risk score, identify who needs a closer look.
  2. Build a traditional ML model in parallel for explainability. Run the same features through Random Forest with SHAP to understand the why.
  3. Loop in HR. Especially for counterintuitive patterns. Data literacy plus domain knowledge turns a prediction into an action.
  4. Don’t treat aggregate results as universal. Attrition drivers for one job family can differ completely from the company-wide pattern.

 

References

  • Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.
  • Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
  • SAP-RPT-1: sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html
  • SAP-RPT-1-OSS: github.com/SAP-samples/sap-rpt-1-oss
  • RPT Playground: rpt.cloud.sap
  • Reference Blog — Carrier Prediction in SAP TM: SAP Community (Mar 2026)

Happy experimenting!

Built with synthetic data mirroring the variable structure and patterns from Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025). No real employee data was used. Results are illustrative.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent
